back_translation: He made a bronze altar, twenty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and ten cubits high.
He cast the Sea of metal, circular in shape, measuring ten cubits from rim to rim, five cubits high, and thirty cubits in circumference.
Below the rim, figures of oxen surrounded the Sea all around, ten per cubit, encircling the Sea; the two rows of oxen were cast as one piece with the Sea.
The Sea stood on twelve oxen: three facing north, three facing west, three facing south, and three facing east. The Sea rested on them, with all their hindquarters toward the center.
The Sea was a handbreadth thick; its rim was made like the brim of a cup, like a lily blossom. It could hold three thousand baths.
He made ten basins for washing, placing five on the right and five on the left, for rinsing the things used in the burnt offering; but the Sea was for the priests to wash in.
He made ten gold lampstands according to the prescribed pattern, and placed them in the temple, five on the right and five on the left.
He made ten tables and placed them in the temple, five on the right and five on the left; and he made a hundred gold sprinkling bowls.
He made the court of the priests and the great court, with doors for the court, and he overlaid the doors with bronze.
He set the Sea on the right side of the house, toward the southeast.
Huram made the pots, shovels, and sprinkling bowls. So Huram finished the work that he had undertaken for King Solomon for the house of God:
the two pillars; the bowls and the capitals on top of the two pillars; the two networks to cover the two bowls of the capitals on top of the pillars;
the four hundred pomegranates for the two networks — two rows of pomegranates for each network — to cover the two bowls of the capitals on top of the pillars;
Huram also made the stands and the basins set on the stands;
the one Sea, and the twelve oxen under it;
the pots, shovels, meat forks, and all the articles. Huram-abi made these for King Solomon for the house of the LORD, all of polished bronze.
The king had them cast in the plain of the Jordan, in molds of clay, between Succoth and Zeredah.
Solomon made all these articles in such great abundance that the weight of the bronze could not be determined.
Solomon made all the articles that were in the house of God: the golden altar, and the tables for the bread of the Presence;
the lampstands with their lamps of pure gold, to burn before the inner sanctuary as prescribed;
the flowers, lamps, and tongs of gold — of the purest gold;

the wick trimmers, sprinkling bowls, dishes, and firepans of pure gold; and the entrance of the house: its inner doors to the Most Holy Place, and the doors of the main hall of the temple, were of gold.
